data.title: Ametek: Highest Margins Among Electrical Components Players
data.description:
By Anthony Goh, Senior Investment Research Analyst @ Khaveen Investments

AMETEK, Inc. (AME) makes precision instruments and motors for aerospace, medical, industrial, and power systems. In this analysis, we covered the company focusing on its profitability to determine why it has higher profit margins than other electrical components peers, with a 5-year average gross margin of 35.12% (peer average 36.26%), an EBIT margin of 24.26% (peer average 15.60%), and a net income margin of 18.87% (peer average 12.59%), and whether it could sustain its superb profitability going forward.

To determine the reasons for AMETEK’s strong profitability, we first examine whether AMETEK’s higher margins are driven by specific business segments by compiling and analyzing the breakdown of its revenue and operating income by segment. We then evaluate whether its focus on niche markets supports higher margins by assessing its competitive positioning. We complete our analysis by examining whether the company's high margins result from an efficient or lean operating model by comparing its cost structure and SG&A efficiency to its peers.

Robust Segment-Level Margins Across Both EIG And EMG Segments

We first examine whether AMETEK’s higher margins are driven by specific business segments by breaking down its revenue and operating income by segment, such as the Electronic Instruments Group (EIG) and Electromechanical Group (EMG), and identify which product lines or sub-segments contribute the most to margins while comparing these results to industry benchmarks and peers.

AMETEK’s business is divided into two main segments: EIG produces advanced analytical and monitoring instruments for aerospace, power, and industrial markets, and EMG focuses on precision motion control and thermal management products. EIG’s main products include spectrometers, calibration instruments, vibration analyzers, and 3D scanners, while EMG’s main products include brushless DC motors, actuators, aerospace-qualified motion components, and thermal management systems.

Electronic Instruments is a leader in the design and manufacture of advanced instruments for the process, power and industrial, and aerospace markets. Electromechanical is a differentiated supplier of precision motion control solutions, highly engineered medical components and devices, thermal management systems, specialty metals and electrical interconnects. Its end markets include aerospace and defense, medical, automation and other industrial markets. – Annual Report

Both are large contributors, with EIG being the larger segment at 67.1% of revenue in 2024 compared to 32.9% for EMG, a split that has remained fairly stable over time. Revenue growth has also been solid across both, averaging about 8.03% annually for EIG and 4.81% for EMG over the period.

In terms of profitability, EIG consistently shows higher operating margins, averaging 26.40%, and has risen slightly from earlier in the period to over 30% in 2024. EMG’s operating margin has averaged 21.66% and was higher from 2021 to 2023, mainly due to price increases as the annual reports frequently cite "pricing actions" or "price initiatives" as drivers, but in 2024 margins moderated as the company’s acquisition of Paragon Medical diluted segment margins according to management disclosures.

Both segments maintain strong operating margins well above typical industry averages, so while EIG’s higher margin clearly supports AMETEK’s overall profitability, EMG also contributes to the higher than industry average margin of the company.

We believe EIG will continue to support AMETEK’s margin growth given its historically higher operating margins compared to EMG. As AMETEK expands EIG through organic growth and acquisitions, we believe the mix shift toward a larger share of EIG is expected to favor overall margin expansion. Between 2015 and 2024, the company’s total operating margin increased by 2.68%, and we calculated that the margin growth is mainly from EIG (2.82% of the increase), whereas the EMG segment is flat (-0.14%). In terms of the operating margin contribution in 2024, the main segment is EIG, contributing 20.58% of the total company's 25.64% operating margin due to the segment being larger (67.1% of revenue) and also having a higher operating margin of 30.65%, while the EMG segment contribution is 6.58% and the corporate elimination margin is -1.52%, totaling 25.64% for the total company.

We believe the market outlook for AMETEK’s EIG and EMG segments is positive, supported by our compiled CAGR estimates for the product markets. EIG markets are projected to grow between 3.9% and 5.9%, while EMG markets range from 4.9% to 6.8%. One of the market growth drivers includes increasing automation and robotics such as the “incorporation of test automation and robotics in test and measurement processes”. To capture these opportunities, the company highlighted in its latest earnings transcript about continued commitment to R&D and new products such as Gatan’s EDAX Elite Ultra spectroscopy system and Vision Research’s Phantom KT-series high-speed cameras designed for industrial and medical applications.

Competitive Positioning In Niche Market Not Main Reason For Top Margins

We next examine whether AMETEK’s focus on niche markets supports its higher margins. As highlighted in its annual report, “AMETEK maintains significant market share in a number of targeted niche markets through its ability to produce and deliver high-quality, differentiated products at competitive prices.”

Compared to other companies in the electrical components sub-industry, AMETEK stands out with the highest operating margin (24.26%), the highest FCF margin (17.68%), and one of the highest net income margins (18.87%), slightly below Emerson Electric’s (EMR) 25.92%, which may be due to gains from divestitures given Emerson’s active M&A strategy as previously highlighted. However, AMETEK’s gross margin of 35.12% is actually below the peer average of 36.26%, indicating its margin strength is not due to exceptionally high product pricing or gross profitability, but rather its ability to convert solid gross margins into industry-leading operating and cash flow margins through strong operational discipline and cost efficiency.

The gross margins of the electrical components subindustry are not particularly high overall, which could be due to its intensive competitiveness, with over 9,000 companies globally, thus indicating a highly competitive market with pricing pressure.

AMETEK holds strong market share in key instrumentation markets such as spectrometry and calibration as Morningstar stated that “AMETEK’s average share in these markets is about 30%-plus, with some of about 50%” as well as “AMETEK remains entrenched in niche, oligopolistic markets with few substitutes and where over 95% of its products compete on differentiation”. But looking at the table, its competitors also have solid competitive positioning in their own focus market segments, such as Schneider in low-voltage switchgear, Rockwell in PLCs, and Emerson in control valves, often holding similar leading shares of 20 to 30% or more in their market segments. We believe this may be one factor why AMETEK’s gross margin is only average at 35.12% versus 36.26%, suggesting that strong market positions and niche focus are not the primary factors driving its higher profitability.

While the company highlights its strong competitive positioning in niche markets, such as holding 30% to 50% share in spectrometry and calibration markets within its largest segment, the EIG (which accounts for 67.1% of revenue), according to Morningstar, we believe this is not the main driver of its superior margins. Given that the company’s gross margin (35.12%) is also in line with the average of its peers (36.26%), we believe this further indicates its competitive positioning is not a major driver, competitors like Schneider Electric (OTCPK:SBGSF), Rockwell Automation (ROK), and Emerson Electric also have solid competitive positions with leading shares in their own specialized product markets. This indicates that AMETEK’s top-tier margins might not be mainly due to unique market share advantages in niche segments. We discuss financial analysis further in the next section to identify the actual reason for its top margins.

Financial Analysis Shows Extremely Efficient SG&A

Since we believe the company's margins are not due to particularly strong differentiation, as its gross margins are only in line with the average of top peers, we then examine what they could be due to, and consider whether they result from lean operations, as its annual report highlights “Efficient and Flexible Manufacturing Operations” as one of its competitive advantages. To do this, we analyze the company's main cost factors with an expense analysis and compare the breakdown of key expenses such as R&D, and SG&A with competitors.

Based on the chart, the largest expense for AMETEK is COGS, which is expected given that its gross margin is around 30%. Beyond COGS, the largest operating expense is SG&A, which is the main overhead cost in the company’s expense structure.

Comparing expenses across peers, AMETEK clearly stands out with the second lowest SG&A margin of 10% versus the peer average of 17.83%. In its annual report, AMETEK highlighted Operational Excellence as its “cornerstone strategy”, focusing on “lean manufacturing, global sourcing, Design for Six Sigma, Value Engineering/Value Analysis, growth kaizens, and digitalization” and that these “Operational Excellence initiatives have yielded lower operating and administrative costs”. But competitors also apply similar strategies. Schneider Electric applies its Schneider Production System to implement lean principles and reduce waste. Eaton (ETN) relies on the Eaton Business System, which integrates Lean, Six Sigma, and value engineering. ABB (OTCPK:ABBNY) uses the ABB Way Operating Model for efficiency improvements. We also believe these strategies primarily reduce manufacturing costs [COGS] and help boost gross margins, which may explain why its gross margins are in line with the industry average as seen in the previous section.

From its annual report, it is stated that “We have over 220 manufacturing locations, sales and service operations in more than 30 countries around the world.” The company reports employing around 22,000 people globally, though it doesn’t break down where those employees are located. When we compare its asset base with top competitors, we see that AMETEK has about 72% of its long-lived assets in the US, higher than peers like Schneider Electric (37%), Eaton (53%), Emerson Electric (53%) and Vertiv (VRT) (41%) so we do not believe it has a relatively larger footprint in lower-cost regions than competitors.

AMETEK also highlights it in its annual report about its decentralized operating structure with individual business units responsible for their own performance. Its sales and marketing are “generally organized and carried out at the business level, combining both its direct sales team and third-party distributors, but this is normal in the industry; competitors like Schneider Electric, Eaton, ABB, Emerson Electric, and Vertiv also rely on both direct sales forces and distributor networks to reach customers worldwide and also highlight decentralized operating models. In terms of R&D, its R&D expense is slightly higher or in line at 5.4% compared to the peer average of 3.7%.

We believe AMETEK’s strong profitability is driven by its low SG&A margin, which makes up a large portion of its operating expenses and has averaged 11% over the past decade. We view its Operational Excellence strategy as not unique, as peers apply similar practices. We believe the margin strength reflects cost discipline and solid execution. SG&A was 10.42% in Q1, showing continued control. We expect SG&A and R&D to remain stable, supporting a 5-year forward net margin of 21.04%, slightly above its historical 19.13%.

Risk: Tariff Uncertainty

We believe one of the risks for AMETEK is tariff uncertainty as the company sources products from outside the US as part of its manufacturing strategy in countries such as China and Malaysia. In its Q1 earnings call, AMETEK management acknowledged increased uncertainty around trade policy and tariffs, which “could cause actual results to differ materially from our current expectations”. While it did not provide guidance on quantified tariff impact, it may lead to a margin impact, but management also noted that while tariffs raise input costs, the company plans to mitigate the impact through “select pricing initiatives, localization of production operations, adjustments to our supply chain and targeted productivity actions”. While the company did not disclose where its products are sourced specifically, its long-lived asset geographic breakdown shows the majority of its assets are in the US (68%) and the remainder is international, with only 1.5% from Asia, so we believe this may not be a major risk.

Verdict

For our projections, we derived 3-year growth forecasts based on the historical average growth of the EIG and EMG segments, estimating 7% total growth through 2027 and tapering our growth assumptions beyond that. Our growth forecasts are also in line with management in its earnings transcript, stating that they “continue to expect full-year sales to be up low single digits on a percentage basis compared to 2024”. For margins, we expect the company to maintain strong profitability, supported by its stable SG&A margin of 10.09% and consistent gross margins, resulting in a 5-year forward average net margin of 21.03%. We valued the company using a DCF model, discounted at the company’s WACC of 9.6%, and benchmarked against the 5-year average multiple of top players in the Electrical Components subindustry of 18.55x.

We see that AMETEK’s overall profitability is strong, supported by robust segment-level margins across both the EIG and EMG segments. While the company highlights its competitive positioning in specialty and niche instrumentation markets, we do not believe this is the main reason for its industry-leading margins, as gross margins are generally strong across competitors and AMETEK’s own gross margin is broadly in line with the peer average. Instead, we conclude that the primary driver of AMETEK’s superior operating margins is its consistently low SG&A expense as a percentage of revenue. We believe this advantage does not stem from any unique structural characteristics but rather could be reflective of the company’s strong management execution and disciplined cost control. Based on our DCF, our valuation shows the company is valued fairly despite its high margins, and also its current forward EV/EBITDA is 19.70x, which is only 3.6% lower than its 5-year historical average of 20.43x, thus we rate the company as a Hold with a price target of $173.08. With the upcoming earnings release on 31 July, we will be paying attention to management guidance on its growth outlook, given its flattish performance in Q1 and indications that it will be able to deliver on its “low single digit” previous, which our forecasts are in line with, as well as any guidance on the tariff impact to assess any profitability impact.

data.title: EU Trade Tides: Front-Loading To U.S. Eases In May
data.description:
As detailed in our EU-US Industrial Trade Vulnerability Dashboard, which tracks the total output at risk of US tariff action, the European Union’s merchandise trade surplus rebounded in May despite a further correction to the front-loading of exports to the US. The announcement of a 30% tariff applicable from Aug. 1 will likely lead to renewed front-loading of deliveries to the US market in July.

The improvement in the trade balance in May compared with the previous month was driven by a combination of a growing surplus in chemicals and other products and a narrowing deficit in energy. The trade balance in the remaining categories worsened in May.

Overall, both extra-EU merchandise exports and imports declined for a second consecutive month in May, but at a slower pace compared with April.

In the lead-up to the Aug. 1 deadline, we expect EU–US trade negotiations to regain momentum, with the European Commission favoring a negotiated solution. Even if these negotiations succeed in reducing the announced 30% levy, the likelihood of EU exporters facing higher US tariffs compared with our current baseline assumptions represents a key downside risk for our real GDP growth forecasts for the second half of 2025 and for 2026.

Trade surplus with the US rises in May as fall in imports outweighs declining exports

The geographical breakdown shows EU exports to the US, which account for over one-fifth of total extra-EU exports, declining by 2% month over month (seasonally and calendar adjusted) in May, following a 30.2% monthly deterioration the previous month related to the announcement of higher US tariffs in early April.

The largest declines in exports to the US occurred in machinery and vehicles (7.3% month-over-month) and other manufactured goods (5.1% month-over-month), with some of the products in this category affected by Section 232 US tariffs. In contrast, EU exports of chemical goods and energy, mostly exempt from the 'reciprocal' US tariffs, increased by 3.8% month-over-month and 1.6% month-over-month, respectively.

Meanwhile, EU imports from the US declined for the third consecutive month, falling by 5.2% month-over-month in May. Overall, the EU trade surplus with the US reached €18.4 billion, widening by more than €0.5 billion since April and by €4.2 billion when compared with May 2024.

While the deficit with mainland China continues to widen

EU goods imports from mainland China increased by 3.4% year-over-year, non-seasonally adjusted terms, in May, while the bloc’s goods exports to mainland China fell by 11.2% year-over-year during the month. The EU’s trade deficit with mainland China increased to €27.5 billion, up from €23.9 billion in May 2024.

Looking at a longer period between March and May 2025, EU imports from mainland China saw an acceleration of 9.5% year-over-year. In contrast, EU exports to mainland China fell by over 12% year-over-year during the same period.

Elsewhere, the May trade data shows an increase in goods imports from other trading partners in Asia. Notably, in March–May 2025, EU imports from Vietnam and Malaysia rose by 19.2% year-over-year and 5.8% year-over-year (NSA), respectively.

The EU market will remain at risk of being exposed to a potential influx of goods imports from trading partners that are being redirected away from the US due to higher tariffs. The impact associated with a potential surge in cheaper imports from Asia is likely to vary by sector.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Stratasys: It Could Stand As A Market Consolidator
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Stratasys (SSYS) is the market leader in the 3D printing industry, having recently surpassed its biggest rival, 3D Systems (DDD), in revenue size. It owns a comprehensive portfolio of 3D printers and materials serving various industries, including aerospace, automotive, and dental applications.

Despite downward cyclical trends affecting clients' purchases of new systems, consumables, and services have been resilient, as can be seen below. In Q1FY25, consumables even grew sequentially by +7% QoQ. The management has stated several times that consumables are expected to increase in FY25 compared to FY24, despite weak capital spending from their clients negatively affecting new system purchases.

Any rate cuts that help industrial PMIs and clients' CAPEX could help to see a rebound in overall activity.

Additionally, SSYS's business can still grow over time if the client mix gradually shifts toward more mass production of parts rather than lower-volume prototyping. This has been confirmed during the last FY24 results:

“our strategy to be laser focused on the most compelling applications, particularly ones that center around full scale production.“ Also, in the Q1FY25: « So we do see the demand. And quarter-by-quarter, of course, we will continue to deliver. And again, it's more and more shifting to manufacturing that has higher utilization and more consumables, of course. »

Profitability has regularly exceeded expectations, thanks to cost-cutting measures and solid pricing, which have helped stabilize gross margins. As a consequence, SSYS has frequently surprised the consensus on the upside by beating earnings-per-share projections.

Over time, when revenue growth bounces back, SSYS is confident to reach 10% plus EBITDA margin levels, as stated during the FY24 results: “in the fourth quarter, we delivered .. adjusted EBITDA and 9.6% margin ... We remain confident that when capital spending constraint is, our operational efficiencies will result in sustainably higher profitability in the coming years. “

In FY23 and FY24, adjusted EBITDA margins were 5.6% (on $627m in sales) and 4.5% (on $572m in sales), respectively, before the cost measures were implemented. As we will see later on, FY25 management forecasts anticipate $47 million of adjusted EBITDA on $580 million in sales, translating into an 8% margin.

On the balance sheet side, SSYS retained a net cash position of $150m in Q1 FY25 before taking into account the $120m capital injection from Fortissimo Capital. The investment firm's managing partner, Yuval Cohen, will join SSYS’s board of directors and bring his 30 years of experience in innovation and technology.

The pro forma net cash position will be $270 million, providing a significant margin of safety and even allowing for a potential budget for M&A in a consolidating market.

During the Q1FY25 conference call, the management insisted that the printing industry should benefit from onshoring trends and protectionism trends : « additive manufacturing is ideally suited as a solution for high tariff environment as it promotes manufacturing locally, quickly and cost effectively. » The CEO also highlighted some recent application cases, including aerospace, where more than 350 3D-printed parts were provided for a jet from Boom Supersonic.

Q2 FY25 results will be released on August 5th. I expect topline trends to remain challenged by weak end markets. Annual revenue guidance implies zero growth, setting the market expectations at a low bar for ongoing quarters. On the other hand, increasing demand and maintaining sound profitability will be key for the stock to perform well, and recent cost improvements lead me to believe that the results will be satisfactory.

What Valuation Can We Expect?

The latest company financial guidance is the following:

I used the following hypotheses:

  • For FY25, I utilized the primary components of the management guidance.
  • Considering the near-term trends, I expect a gradual V-shape recovery of revenues toward $650m by FY25 while EBITDA margins converge close to 10%, in line with recent management comments.
  • Longer term: I assume a gradual gain of gross margins as highly profitable consumables will grow in the mix. The topline is modeled at 5% sales growth per year, higher than GDP growth, as 3D printing could benefit from increasing mass manufacturing (gaining share versus traditional ways of producing parts).

With a terminal growth of 3% and a WACC of 10%, I get a target price of $16/share, indicating a 40% upside potential.

Risks

The 3D industry has long been a valley of disillusionment for investors. I can list a couple of reasons explaining disappointing growth rates and value destruction for shareholders. First, too many firms have been involved in the field, having to compete on price as barriers to entry were lower than expected. Secondly, clients have thus far failed to transition from prototyping to mass production of 3D printing parts. Finally, a succession of macroeconomic stresses, including the supply chain crisis and the current phase of elevated interest rates, hurt economic trends and the industry.

As a consequence, the sector has been loss-making for years, with some players falling into bankruptcy. Will SSYS stand in the storm and act as a market consolidator? Given its competitive portfolio, highly net cash balance sheet, and restored profitability, there is a chance.

Conclusion

SSYS has been conservatively managed over recent years, focusing on a market that could eventually enter mass production over time, while controlling the pace of spending and preserving its balance sheet. With the recent $120m cash injection, SSYS's financials are further solidified. A DCF model, assuming above 10% EBITDA margins and 5% midterm revenue growth, indicates that there is still upside potential. This reinforces my buy rating on the stock.

data.title: Unleveraged Options In The Tax-Exempt Sector
data.description:
One of the key allocation decisions for income investors is how much risk exposure to take in order to achieve their goals. Risk exposure can encompass everything from asset classes and individual securities to the desired level of diversification to the types of investment vehicles and much more. One decision that can be overlooked is how much fund-level leverage to take. In this article, we take a closer look at how investors can think about this type of leverage and make the right decision that works best for them.

What is Fund-Level Leverage?

There are different types of financial leverage. There is operating leverage on the corporate level, which has to do with how profits change relative to revenues. There is corporate leverage on the balance sheet, which has to do with how much debt a company carries relative to its equity. There is also individual leverage that investors may have via margin lending. There is also structural leverage within asset classes such as CLOs, where CLO equity holders carry leverage by virtue of how CLOs are put together.

The leverage that we look at here is fund-level leverage, which is leverage within a fund such as a CEF or a BDC. For example, a fund that has $500m of total assets and $200m of borrowings has leverage of 40% in CEF parlance and 0.67x in BDC parlance.

Fund-level leverage is reported by CEFs and BDCs periodically. For example, this is what the Nuveen Quality Muni Income Fund (NAD) reports.

In previous articles we have discussed sub-types of fund-level leverage such as regulatory leverage, trading leverage, synthetic leverage, and more, however these distinctions are not as relevant here. Subscribers can find the relevant discussion in our CEF Guide.

Fund-Level Leverage Decision Factors

The decision around taking exposure to fund-level leverage comes down to the two usual considerations about allocating to income assets, which is carry or income and capital gains - the two components of total return. In this section, we take a look at the thought process using High Yield corporate bonds.

Let's take carry first. At the moment, the weighted-average yield of the HY corporate bond market is 6.89%. A typical CEF that allocates to corporate bonds will have a third of its assets financed with leverage, i.e., it has 35% leverage. An important question is: how much carry or yield does the fund generate on its unleveraged and leveraged assets?

Credit CEFs typically charge around 0.7-1.2% in total fees. If we use 1% as a rough guide, then our CEF passes on around 5.9% in carrying on its unleveraged assets (i.e. those financed with equity). As far as its leveraged assets, we also need to take into account the cost of leverage, which, for most credit CEFs, will be around 3m term SOFR + 0.85% or so, which works out to 5.15% today. Adding the 5.15% cost of leverage to the fee of 1% gets us to 6.15%. Taking that out of the base yield of 6.89% leaves us with 0.74%.

In short, a common shareholder of the fund will take home 5.9% on the fund's unleveraged assets (around 2/3 of the fund) and around 0.74% of the fund's leveraged assets. Keep in mind we are not taking other costs into account such as trading costs, credit losses, etc., nor are we taking into account the benefit of the fund discount, if any.

This simple exercise will differ by asset class. The higher the quality of the asset class, the less the investors will take home on a fund's leveraged assets. For instance, investment-grade and tax-exempt fund shareholders are currently making next to nothing on the leveraged portion of their CEF assets. This is largely because the credit spreads of these assets are lower than that of high-yield corporate bonds.

Apart from the question of carry, there is also the question of capital gains. Fund-level leverage increases the impact of price changes in the underlying assets on the fund's NAV. For example, our 35% leveraged CEF will have a beta that is 50% higher than that of the underlying index because each $10 of NAV has $15 of bond exposure.

The motivation here is that investors who are extremely bullish on a given asset class do not care as much about the carry component as they are looking to make money from the rise in the price of the underlying asset. A larger carry is nice, but it's not essential to the allocation decision. And vice-versa, an investor who is extremely defensive will want to take as little leverage as possible to avoid the magnified downside even if the carry is very attractive.

For leveraged funds like CEFs and BDCs, capital gains can work on two levels - underlying assets and fund discounts. Both wide credit spreads of underlying assets and wide discounts make leverage attractive, as there is greater potential for large capital gains than when credit spreads and discounts are tight.

To review, fund-level leverage is attractive when carry on leveraged assets is relatively high and/or when the potential for capital gains is high. To ground the discussion in today's market environment, let's take a look at where we are on this front.

The level of carry on leveraged assets is a function of two things - credit spreads and the shape of the yield curve. The wider the credit spreads and the steeper the yield curve, the more carry leveraged assets will typically generate net of all costs. Today credit spreads are pretty tight, and the relevant Treasury yield curve is inverted. What this means is that, from a carry perspective, fund leverage is not very attractive.

Let's turn to capital gains. Fund-level leverage is attractive from a capital gain perspective when credit spreads and discounts are wide. As highlighted above, credit spreads are tight, and so are discounts. What this suggests is that fund-level leverage for a typical credit CEF is also unattractive from a capital gain perspective.

Tax-Exempt Sector Drilldown

Let's see how this plays out in the tax-exempt sector and what investors can do in an environment where leveraged exposure is less attractive. An investor asked us recently if leveraged Muni CEFs are delivering pretty much the same level of portfolio yield (not to be confused with distribution rate) as unleveraged ones, why does a leveraged CEF like NAD have an NAV yield of 4.2% but an unleveraged one like NIM have a NAV yield of 3.4%.

The answer lies with the duration profile of the two funds. NIM has a duration of 5.2, while NAD has a duration of 15.8. Even adjusting for the leverage, NAD has double the duration of NIM.

Tax-exempt yield curves below show that, unlike for Treasuries, longer-duration tax-exempt bonds offer significantly more yield than shorter-duration bonds. In short, NAD is delivering more yield not because it carries leverage but because it holds longer-duration bonds.

That said, because higher-quality tax-exempt bonds (i.e., those that are held by the vast majority of tax-exempt CEFs) trade at tight credit spreads, there is not enough additional yield to overcome the combination of fund fees and the cost of leverage.

Investors who want to achieve a similar level of yield as leveraged tax-exempt CEFs without taking on leverage should simply look at longer-duration unleveraged CEFs like the Nuveen AMT-Free Muni Value Fund (NUW) with an NII yield of 3.9%.

Alternatively, they can explicitly focus on longer-dated bonds via ETFs like the VanEck Long Muni ETF (MLN) and the iShares Long-Term National Muni Bond ETF (LMUB). These two ETFs have net portfolio yields (i.e., portfolio yield-to-worst less management fees) of 4.7% and 4.5%, respectively - higher than that of NAD. Another option is to look at High-Yield tax-exempt ETFs such as the SPDR Nuveen ICE High Yield Muni Bond ETF (HYMB) which has a net portfolio yield of 5.15%.

Analyzing when to take exposure to fund-level leverage is one of the key allocation decisions investors should take. Rather than assume that more leverage is always better, a judicious exposure to fund-level leverage should allow investors to reach their investment goals without taking on excessive risk.

data.title: AMD: The King Of Inference
data.description:
I bought back into AMD (NASDAQ:AMD) again in early May and haven't stopped since. AMD described as 'volatile' is nothing short of the truth, with a beta of ~2, as positive/negative sentiments lead to larger swings. Over the last few years, AMD has trailed Nvidia in terms of GPU power, as Nvidia's revenue has significantly outpaced AMD's. Nvidia's ecosystem of better training performance generated the majority of their revenue (data center), which was the reason why Nvidia's revenue significantly outpaced AMD. However, AMD is silently and slowly catching up, now focusing on the next biggest fast-growing segment for GPUs, inference. From the CEO Lisa Su herself saying she expects inference to have a CAGR of 80% (see graph below). AMD GPU is better compared to Nvidia's in respect to inference, which is now AMD's main focus, rather than training AI models.

AMD is still underperforming many major indexes, such as S&P 500 and QQQ, over the past year, as overhype has led to AMD being previously overvalued. Now with their new line of products with MI400 Series GPUs being available in 2026 is an absolute game changer, fully capitalizing on the increased demand for inference as well as significantly narrowing the tech gap with Nvidia. With Nvidia having a market cap of ~$4.1T and AMD only having a market cap of ~$255B, the gap in market cap doesn't reflect how close AMD is getting to Nvidia. Hence, I'm initiating AMD at a 'strong buy'.

Thesis

Lisa Su has done phenomenal work at AMD, bringing AMD from a company struggling for any relevance to now surpassing rivals Intel in market cap with Nvidia in sight. This is not the first time AMD has been the underdog. AMD surpassing Nvidia in market cap in the next few years is highly unlikely, but the gap is certainly shrinking. AMD in the past year has been laughed at by investors with names like 'Advanced Money Destroyer' as Wall Street analysts price targets kept falling alongside AMD's stock price, but now the reverse is happening. Optimism in the market, besting Nvidia on AI inference, collaborations with the Middle East, and OpenAI preference on AMD's GPUs, has led to a rebound and higher priced targets raised by Wall Street. With global investment in AI infrastructure only increasing, as McKinsey estimates the number to be as high as $7 trillion by 2030. AI inference is needed in this technological boom. If AMD has a better product for this specific element in their GPUs, AMD significantly closes the gap, as customers will want AMD GPUs for inference rather than training.

With AMD's estimates for 2028 of a TAM of $500 billion (see graph below) with a larger share for inference, AMD is positioning itself for the future for inference rather than competing with Nvidia now for training. Their recent AI-advancing keynote improvements, as well as OpenAI adoption of the MI400X, are all super optimistic for AMD, as AMD growth is soaring with the removal of China restrictions as well.

AMD Inference Development

AI inference is the process that a trained machine learning model uses to draw conclusions from brand-new data. So Nvidia dominates at training AI, which involves learning from massive datasets, in contrast to inference, which is to make predictions on new data. From the AI advancing keynote event, it has been evident that this is what AMD is focused on, with the improvements of MI355x compared to MI300x of up to 35x. With MI400 coming out in 2026 with even better technology and adopted by OpenAI, this is just the tip of the iceberg on AMD's amazing product.

The new MI355x launched on 6/12/2025 and is an absolute game-changer in the competition in the GPU market. I'm going to break down why the better stats in memory capacity and memory bandwidth are essential to win the race for AI inference.

Memory bandwidth is important because LLMs require GPUs to repeatedly fetch weights from memory after drawing new conclusions and then perform operations in matrix multipliers to push intermediate output back and forth or activations. To simplify, a GPU isn't always CPU-bound, which is 'time it takes for it to complete is determined principally by the speed of the central processor', but simply waits for the data to move across its memory. Therefore, it is memory-bound, as performance is determined by how fast data can move across memory channels.

Tokens are units of data processed by AI models during training and inference to enable predictions and reasoning. In transformer models like GPT, each token fetches weights from memory for each transformer, performs matrix multipliers, then stores intermediate activations. 10s of GBs of memory traffic per token is generated, as a lower bandwidth leads to being laggy/choppy for users when an answer is generated. The higher the bandwidth, questions get answered faster as data is received faster, since each token is generated independently but all require the same GPU memory bandwidth. GPU cycles hence stay fully utilized with a lower latency, as memory lines will always stay open, leading to lower cost per user. A higher token/second generates results in higher margins for data center operators, as all queries are served efficiently, rapidly, and simultaneously. Higher bandwidth simply transfers the queries to GPU cores faster, and with no idle cores, token/user is lower.

LLMs would hence want a combination of good bandwidth and storage, as operating costs would be lower and queue time and jitter would be lower for queries. MI355X excels as it produces 40% more tokens per dollar than an Nvidia GPU, as this leads to lower operating income for operators as variable cost is lower. In this sense, AMD is competing against Nvidia on cost, as AMD's chips will cost less to operate due to lower power consumption and more tokens/dollar, undercutting Nvidia's aggressive pricing by having probably the most wanted product in the world. With the launch of the MI400 in as soon as 2026, AMD is gaining market share by how much they are more cost-effective than Nvidia's chip, no longer being the 2nd choice to Nvidia for operators who may want lower costs.

Higher memory capacity is probably the most important element, as it enables single-GPU inference, so it avoids splitting models across multiple GPUs. This means that LLMs will use model parallelism, which is to distribute the machine learning models feeding neural networks across multiple devices since there is not enough memory capacity. This would add latency across GPUs since there is data hopping, requiring more and more infrastructure and the complexities with software. High latency leads to higher cost and slower inference, which could lead to failure of the whole model itself.

The entire model itself for the large language models (LLM), such as GPT'4 and Llama, requires hundreds or thousands of GB to run. E.g., with GPT-4 estimated to have 1.8 trillion parameters, typically using fp16 (half-precision point format of 16 bits) for calculating the memory size required, it would be 1.8 trillion x 2 bytes, equaling a minimum of around 3350GB. Calculation: 1 GB = 1,073,741,824 bytes. 1.8T x 2 bytes = 3.6T bytes. So converting back to GB of 3.6T/byte equals approximately 3350GB (do remember that the 1.8T parameter was estimated and only a rough number). This would require more Nvidia GPUs compared to AMD's, being more costly and increasing latency from model sharding.

More GPUs/hardware leads to more setup, making it harder to manage power, cooling, and rack space, which will increase with model parallelism. More GPUs required lead to higher infrastructure costs and lower efficiency, as maintaining all GPUs to be perfectly utilized in a shared setup is difficult (more load imbalances and higher latency). With a higher memory capacity, it bests Nvidia in terms of inference, as fewer GPUs are required and lower latency is more attractive for inference. The ability to keep up with Nvidia in terms of bandwidth and higher in terms of memory storage capacity is essential, as higher bandwidth = lower costs per token from users = higher margins for the operators. Higher memory capacity = fewer GPUs = less latency = faster computation = tokens generated faster. This shows that AMD's product may not be that behind Nvidia's compared to both market caps, as AMD's chip might start getting large orders for its effectiveness in AI inference.

The real game changer is in the MI400, which has a memory capacity of 432GB, a jump from 288GB in their MI355X, and a bandwidth of 19.6TB/s, a jump from 8TB/s, all very impressive improvements. Though the chip itself is expected to launch in 2026, there are already big customers. Sam Altman appeared on stage during their Advancing AI 2025 conference, as OpenAI would use AMD chips. This shows that AMD can keep up with Nvidia, as is evident with the trust of OpenAI.

ChatGPT is still the most used AI app worldwide, with hundreds of millions of users, which will require the best inference on the market to maintain the gap between ChatGPT and other LLMs. Having a big client simply creates trust within the product, considering AMD is the underdog, as other LLMs will also start using AMD, such as Meta's Llama. With OpenAI GPT-4's constant growth and improving on their product, AMD simply benefits from being the supplier for the biggest LLM in the world. From the AI Advancing keynote as well, it was also noted that the MI400 series will have 10x more performance than MI355 (slide 98). With the MI355X only released recently and the MI400 in 2026, these are impressive improvements for AMD.

The big part in this is also their Helios AI rack, which, from the AI advancing keynote event, will be one of the most highly advanced for at-scale training and distributed inference. Though MI400 here is the main attraction, it has also been noted that Helios is AMD's first-ever rack that they fully architected as a united system, combining all of AMD's in-house CPUs, GPUs, Pensando NICs, and ROCm software on one platform. This shows significant improvement from prior AI racks, which took parts from third-party or partner components, likely from a server vendor like Dell. With every single component made off of AMD products, Helios shows that AMD is controlling their entire hardware and software design, easing factors like deployment and potentially efficiency, also rivaling Nvidia's DGX.

My optimism is mainly in the growth of inference and the data center AI accelerator as a whole. With inference at a CAGR of 80% from 2023-2028 and an over 60% CAGR for training and inference, this level of growth is simply impressive, and AMD is well-positioned. With Lisa Su predicting the AI processor market achieving $500B by 2028, with inference having a larger proportion of revenue than training, Lisa has the vision for the longer term, as training revenue now may top inference, but inference in the future will top training, and AMD is positioning itself to challenge Nvidia for market share in inference.

It's now all about execution for AMD. MI400 already has huge clients, such as OpenAI creating orders for AMD, before it even fully releases in 2026. On its current trajectory, it expects MI500 in 2027 after MI400 in 2026. Judging from the huge improvements in memory storage and bandwidth from MI355x to MI400, huge improvements from MI400 to MI500 will also be highly likely.

AMD's huge client list

AMD in terms of AI training may not be able to compete with Nvidia, but their clients for AI inference aren't just small firms but rather many big names. Meta (Llama inference), Microsoft/OpenAI (GPT-4 inference), Amazon, Oracle, and Google (EPYC CPUs), just to name a few, with even more like Tesla and Apple. With deals like the $10 billion Saudi AI partnership with AMD, which is a multi-year collaboration to advance global AI infrastructure. Why this deal is significant to me is to show that AMD isn't getting left out of such massive deals with the US and Middle East, which can have evidently multi-billion-dollar contracts, and potential future contracts if it all goes well and sales don't get blocked. Rivals Nvidia is also obviously involved and plays a big part, supplying Blackwell to Saudi Arabia, but AMD also contracted to supply to Saudi Arabia is also a brilliant start, with $10 billion over the next 5 years. On a per-year basis of $2 billion, it does make up for the losses in Chinese export restrictions of expected $1.5 billion in 2025, but the Humain deal will balance it out.

Intel vs AMD CPU

AMD has been slowly taking Intel's annual CPU market share, from its low point of 11% in 2014 to 24.7% in 2025 so far, with estimates that AMD will eventually get to ~39.2% in 2029.

AMD's next-gen 2 nm 5th-gen EPYC CPUs launched on April 23, marking the industry's first HPM CPU on 2 nm production with TSMC. These are their new Zen 5 series.

These chips are already powering Google Cloud, which signals to investors that AMD is growing in dominance in the cloud space. Other cloud services, such as Microsoft, Google, and Oracle also now run on EPYC CPUs.

Why this is significant is because the gap AMD is creating with EPYC and Intel Xeon CPUs is only getting bigger. With the current CPU 'Turin' using their Zen 5c chips already significantly outperforming Intel Xeon 6s metrics, such as general-purpose computing, server-side Java, power efficiency, web servers, in-memory analytics, media processing and many more.

The gap is only increasing, as Venice also has massive upgrades coming out in as soon as 2026. Example, Turin can compute up to 192 cores with 3/4nm Zen cores, leading to being more efficient and faster than Venice can compute up to 256 cores with a 2nm Zen core. All elements of the CPU will be computing faster, as Venice will be significantly better than Turin. As Turin is already ahead of Intel's Xeon 6 by many elements, AMD will simply keep winning market share as Intel will keep losing. AMD is no longer the underdog but is starting to be the leader, especially since big cloud providers like Google are using AMD's EPYC processors.

Although Intel still has a majority market share right now in the CPU market, AMD will keep gaining ground with its better products. Here are a few reasons why Intel still has the majority market share:

Intel has dominated CPUs for decades and has decade-long relationships with original equipment manufacturers (OEM) like Dell, HP and Lenovo, government, and enterprises. Due to their reliability, major entities will have contracts with Intel years before as their product used to be somewhat similar to AMD's, hence winning over contracts.

Intel operates as an Integrated Device Manufacturer(IDM), as it designs and manufactures its CPUs. Compared to AMD, which is fabless and relies on TSMC. Their ability to scale is their key to win, as it can ship a lot more than what AMD can ship due to being fabless, with their goal of 100 million units of its Intel Core Ultra family of processors that include an NPU by the end of 2025, estimated to be 5x more than what Qualcomm and AMD combined will ship. However, it can only be evaluated if the 100 million units are accurate by the end of the year. Hence, Intel is not trying to win by pure performance of the CPU itself, but making sure it has software developers on its side and that new AI workloads work best on Intel's platform rather than AMD's.

With a more mature software ecosystem, it is important as it enables and optimizes AI workloads for firms. This suggests that enterprise applications are optimized to run best on Intel platforms, as the software ecosystem for these firms is already well established, and they also have enough CPUs to actually connect it all. This is where AMD is worst at, which is their software ecosystem of ROCm. Though heavily improving, major clients like Microsoft and Adobe will still prefer to utilize Intel's software, as this is Intel's goal to retain their CPU clients, as they can scale their product. AMD CPU performance is still no doubt better, constantly innovated to be a better product (3/4 nm to 2nm) with better performance, as overtime AMD will slowly catch up with more clients and TSMC having a more established Arizona fab, as is only a matter of time for AMD to catch up to Intel in terms of scale (waiting for TSMC Arizona fab to be fully operational).

AMD has consistently taken Intel's market share and will keep doing so, estimated to be as high as ~39.2% in 2029, as AMD is no longer the underdog. Though Intel can largely scale its product and gain more market share, it is only a matter of time for AMD to scale as well and gain Intel's customers, as AMD's CPUs have better performance compared to Intel's, as evident in Google Cloud using EPYC CPUs to run demanding HPC workloads.

Nvidia vs AMD software

Nvidia has more developed software compared to AMD, with its closed-source CUDA compared to AMD's open-source ROCm. Why this matters is that being closed source, CUDA is a proprietary platform owned by Nvidia with no customization, as it is easier to deploy as the process has been streamlined by Nvidia. Its performance also significantly outpaces ROCm, usually by 20%+. With domination over the market, and their product already being the best, enterprises wouldn't prioritize reimplementing to ROCm, as why switch to a less inferior product?

In terms of performance and ecosystem maturity, no doubt Nvidia is better. However, ROCm is also improving and has its own attractive points. Being open source, it gives developers flexibility in how they deploy the platform, such as modifying and optimizing ROCm to better suit their needs, such as including additional encryption, optimizing inference latency and power efficiency. This simply reduces overhead costs that may be involved and is more suitable for custom solutions or optimizing specific AI workloads.

As also listed in the AI advancing keynote, the new ROCm 7 has an average 3.5x performance improvement in terms of inference and a 3x performance improvement in terms of training compared to ROCm 6, and it just came out. It's not about how Nvidia is better, but rather how AMD is catching up. Slowly catching up in what Nvidia already does best and ahead of the game of in 'training', and also focusing on 'inference', which Lisa Su believes to be bigger than training in the future as seen above, is how AMD aims to take market share in the GPU market. AMD is creating a selling point for customers that want the open source, whilst still slowly catching up to Nvidia.

Additional further optimism

AMD's board also authorized an additional $6 billion of share buybacks, and now the total is $10B. This directly reflects the board's confidence in AMD's direction and their prospects under Lisa Su, as their consistently strong FCF growth allows this. This also increases shareholders confidence in AMD as well as AMD showing that they care about their investors.

Llama is a huge customer for AMD, using their open source to innovate, such as Llama Stack. Meta has used AMD's GPU for inference, using MI300X. Meta is currently recruiting a bunch of talented AI individuals from OpenAI as well as Apple's head of AI model to create the new division called Meta Superintelligence Labs. Zuckerberg said that this team will work on foundation models such as the open-source Llama software, as this signals that Llama models are only getting larger, hopefully soon with a roadmap, as this will require more of AMD's GPUs. This is a big project for Meta, with Zuckerberg spending hundreds of millions in this recruitment frenzy. There are huge expectations that Meta will focus on LLMs as their recent open-source Llama 4 model had a bad reception. This simply increases demand for AMD's GPUs.

Earnings call

Data center was up 57% YoY, client up 28% YoY are all impressive numbers in Q1. What to look out for now, noted in the transcript is 'we remain confident we can deliver strong double-digit-percentage revenue growth in 2025 based on accelerating share gains with our latest generation of Zen 5 EPYC and Ryzen CPUs and Radeon GPUs'.

Focusing on their revenue growth, this was expected without the China market, as revenue from the loss China market was to be made up with the Saudi deal. This is no longer the case, as the $1.5billion set back is no longer the case, as if the revenue is restored, this can be evident if AMD raises guidance.

This is key to focus on, as TSMC earnings call said 'we did not receive the signal yet. So it's too early to give you an estimate' from Nvidia for their H20 chips ban being uplifted. With AMD releasing earnings before Nvidia, it is key to note in AMD's earnings whether AMD has actually benefited from the ban uplift of their MI308 chips. The next earnings call is important for the rest of the year, as it sets the stage of demand for their new line of products, the extent of the negative effects of this US-China tension, and whether the loss revenue can be restored, as the Saudi deal no longer makes up for loss revenue but is now considered as new revenue.

Risks

Similar to Nvidia, AMD is fabless. This creates a reliance on TSMC for their fabs to manufacture for AMD. TSMC is at risk of a China invasion, which could heavily affect supply from TSMC, whose demand currently outweighs supply. This isn't an AMD-specific issue, as the whole AI sector will get affected, but still a risk to think about.

Another is execution risk. AMD needs to be able to execute to have a GPU better in inference than Nvidia, whilst competing in CPUs with Intel. In this AI race, clients(hyperscalers) simply want the best product on the market, as whichever GPU or CPU is better for their operations will have higher demand. Nvidia has dominated the GPU space for years while AMD plays catch-up. Despite the high optimism in inference, I have for AMD that it will dominate in inference, the risk of Nvidia showing a better product is not impossible.

Valuation

AMD has surged ~20% over the last month, and trades at a higher P/E than most of its peers, including Nvidia. AMD trades at a forward P/E of ~78, whilst Nvidia trades at a forward P/E of ~42.

AMD is relatively flat on a 1Y basis with a significantly lower market cap than Nvidia. If AMD proves that it can dominate in inference, it can turn out to be a huge winner.

Through a slide from above shows that TAM in 2023 was $45 billion, AMD captured $6.5 billion, which is 14.44%. With an expected TAM of $500 billion by 2028, this would sit at an approximate revenue of $72.2 billion for data center revenue alone in 2028. If inference gets larger than training and AMD does successfully dominate inference as suggested in the graph, AMD's share could be way larger than $72.2 billion. At a market cap of only $250B, this seems like an excellent buying opportunity for such a dominant AI player.

Conclusion

AMD is not trying to beat Nvidia's moat in AI training, but rather positioning itself in what it believes is the next big market, inference. The AMD client list is also getting larger as contracts from OpenAI, Saudi Arabia, and potential new China ones increase demand for AMD GPUs. Is now all on execution, as its competitor Nvidia's roadmap is nothing short of impressive, as it needs to have a unique selling point/function to keep up with Nvidia, which its inference is showing. Also to note, their next earnings call is on Aug. 5, which is less than 2 weeks. AMD will be very volatile as optimism might rise or fade. If AMD reassures its MI400X release as well as its loss of revenue from China being brought back, the future for AMD is very optimistic.

data.title: Starvine Capital H1 2025 Partner Letter
data.description:
Dear Partner,

During H1 2025, fully-invested accounts in the Starvine Flagship Strategy increased 5.0% to 6.5%, while fully-invested accounts in the Mid-Large Cap Strategy increased 3.9% to 4.2%. During the period, the S&P TSX Total Return Index1 increased 10.2% and the S&P 500 ((SP500), (SPX)) Total Return Index increased 0.8% in Canadian dollars (5.5% in USD).

Annualized results to June 30, 2025 Flagship Mid-Large Cap
Inception date Feb.10,2015 Mar. 16, 2017
Trailing:
One year 45.7% 41.4%
Two Years 26.9% 26.5%
Three Years 26.6% 23.9%
Four Years 12.8% 11.9%
Five Years 21.8% 20.4%
Six Years 16.4% 15.4%
Seven Years 13.0% 12.8%
Eight Years 13.0% 12.2%
Nine Years 14.5% n/ a
Ten Years 11.3% n/a
Since Inception 10.6% 12.1%
The composite figures above are unaudited and include discretionary fee-paying accounts within each respective mandate. Composite results are presented as time-weighted rates of return, net of management fees and other expenses. Results will vary with subscription date, most notably for recently formed accounts whose position weightings and cash levels tend to differ initially from the investment strategies. Clients will therefore each have their own net-of-all fees performance results from investing in Starvine. References made to indices may provide clients with a benchmark to compare results. However, the Starvine strategies are operated with a bottom-up selection framework and thus no effort is committed to tracking any index. Lastly,the composite results displayed above should not be interpreted as a reference for future returns.
Calendar year results: Flagship Mid-Large Cap
Inception date Feb. 10, 2015 Mar. 16, 2017
2025- year to date 5.6% 4.0%
2024 52.7% 50.4%
2023 13.5% 15.2%
2022 $-18.8%$ -21.0%
2021 49.0% 45.3%
2020 5.1% 6.2%
2019 19.9% 21.4%
2018 -6.7% $-6.6%$
2017 14.5% 3.9%
2016 $4.2%$ n/a
2015 -8.4% n/a

Commentary

The first half of 2025 brought the steepest market drawdown since the onset of COVID in March 2020. Starvine strategies were not spared. This time, the volatility stemmed from uncertainty surrounding trade tariff proposals from the Trump administration. Yet, much like in 2020, the downturn reversed quickly-markets rebounded within just two months.

Currency movements also had a material impact. The weakening of the U.S. dollar relative to the Canadian dollar detracted approximately 3.5% and 3.2% from returns in the Flagship and Mid-Large Cap strategies, respectively.

While the tariff headlines were unsettling, the businesses we own were largely insulated in terms of free cash flow exposure. This was already evident as the situation unfolded in early 2025 and further validated during Q1 earnings calls in April and May, where management teams reiterated the limited impact.

Ten Years In- Reflections and Evolution

Now that Starvine has crossed the 10-year milestone, I'd like to share some reflections.

Since inception, the Flagship strategy has posted three down years: 2015, 2018, and 2022. These years have weighed on the cumulative number but returns remain firmly in double-digit territory. Notably, the strongest performance came during the back half of the decade, highlighted by standout years in 2021 and 2024, though tempered by the sharp pullback in 2022.

Evaluating any investment track record is complex, as it's difficult to cleanly separate skill from luck. A professional athlete may deliver a few exceptional seasons, but that doesn't ensure another great year is coming. Still, the athlete trains relentlessly, seeking improvement within their control. Similarly, we as investors cannot predict how any given year will unfold-but we can commit to sharpening our analytical skills, strengthening our investment philosophy, and managing behavior with discipline.

Even so, doing all the “right” things- sticking to a long-term approach and focusing on high-quality businesses-does not guarantee smooth results. There will still be rough years. Meanwhile, it's possible to see good results from poor habits like chasing trends or succumbing to FOMO. Over time, however, the process wins out.

In hindsight, the first five years (2015-2020) could have been stronger. One area that weighed on returns was my overenthusiasm for spin-offs. The thesis was that forced selling could lead to mispricing and opportunity. That still holds true in certain cases, and some spin-offs have worked well for us. But in those early years, I occasionally prioritized the situation over the business, which led to two key mistakes: (1) investing in a few sub-par companies, and (2) lacking conviction in some holdings that later became big winners after I exited.

A key turning point came in 2017, when I introduced a basic checklist that placed greater emphasis on fundamental business quality rather than relying too heavily on valuation-driven setups. While valuation (the price paid relative to intrinsic value) remains important, I came to view it as just one part of a broader mosaic formed from imperfect information. A useful analogy is picking a cashier line at the grocery store: choosing the shortest line doesn't always get you out faster. Sometimes, opting for the longer line (i.e., paying a higher valuation multiple) is wiser if it's moving more quickly-akin to investing in a business with strong earnings growth. Conversely, a short line (i.e., a cheap stock) might be a value trap- perhaps the cashier is slow, and others are avoiding it for good reason. Overweighting price while neglecting quality can lead to suboptimal outcomes.

This shift in mindset helped frame my view of 2022-a year that looked poor on paper, especially due to sentiment turning against alternative asset managers amid rising interest rates. Yet fundamentally, these businesses continued to grow, ultimately validating the investment theses. Using the previous analogy, the cashier line kept moving at a steady pace in 2022, but customers avoided it on the expectation that it would halt.

Since then, the philosophy has further evolved: from seeking “great returns” to prioritizing durable compounding. This has led to a deeper appreciation for the rarity and resilience of certain portfolio holdings. While there will always be some level of turnover- due to risk management, capital reallocation, or thesis violations-I expect a meaningful portion of the portfolio to remain long-term holdings.

Closing Thoughts

I often draw parallels between investing and athletics: both require discipline, continuous learning, and a mindset focused on the process, not the outcome. The key difference, however, is that investing allows for cumulative improvement over a lifetime-as long as one's thinking remains sharp. Experience compounds just like capital.

That said, the work remains as dynamic as ever. I am constantly assessing new ideas, re-evaluating existing holdings, managing position sizes, and staying vigilant for potential value traps.

Thank you for your continued engagement.

Sincerely,

Steven Ko, Portfolio Manager

DISCLAIMER

Readers are advised that the material herein should be used solely for informational purposes. Starvine Capital Corporation (SCC”) does not purport to tell or suggest which investment securities members or readers should buy or sell for themselves. Readers should always conduct their own research and due diligence and obtain professional advice before making any investment decision. SCC will not be liable for any loss or damage caused by a reader's reliance on information obtained in any of our newsletters, presentations, special reports, email correspondence, or on our website. Readers are solely responsible for their own investment decisions. The information contained herein does not constitute a representation by the publisher or a solicitation for the purchase or sale of securities. Our opinions and analyses are based on sources believed to be reliable and are written in good faith, but no representation or warranty, expressed or implied, is made as to their accuracy or completeness. All information contained in our newsletters, presentations or on our website should be independently verified with the companies mentioned. The editor and publisher are not responsible for errors or omissions. Past performance does not guarantee future results. Investment returns will fluctuate and there is no assurance that a client's account can maintain a specific net liquidation value. The S&P 500 Total Return Index and the S&P/TSX Composite Total Return Index (the indexes") are similar to Starvine's investment strategy in that all include should be independently verified with the companies mentioned. The editor and publisher are not responsible for errors prices as well as reinvestment of dividend income. However, there are several differences between Starvine's investment strategy and the indexes, as Starvine can take concentrated positions in single equities, and may invest in companies that have smaller market capitalizations than those that are included in the indexes. In addition, the indexes do not include any fees or expenses whereas the return data presented is net of all fees and expenses. SCC receives no compensation of any kind from any companies that are mentioned in our newsletters or on our website. Any opinions expressed are subject to change without notice. The Starvine investment strategy and other related parties may hold positions in the securities that are discussed in the newsletters, presentations or on the company website.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Arquitos Capital Management Q2 2025 Investor Letter
data.description:
Dear Partner,

Arquitos returned 13.1% net of fees in the second quarter of 2025, bringing the year-to-date return to 28.8%. Individual returns will vary based on timing of investment. Please check your statement for specific results. See page four for more detailed performance information.

Uncertainty has always been part of the human experience. For most of history, the unknown posed existential threats, whether from predators, disease, or rival tribes. To survive, we developed a deep psychological aversion to uncertainty. We overcorrected for danger, defaulting to caution, and passed those instincts forward through generations.

Storytelling became our way of managing that uncertainty. Stories helped us make sense of the world, coordinate as groups, and feel safe, even when that sense of safety was built on illusion. Today, those same instincts remain. When people face ambiguity, they often construct narratives that provide comfort rather than clarity. “Unknown” is intolerable, so it must be filled in. Truth is less important.

This tendency is especially visible in markets. Investing is an extension of human nature and is highly susceptible to narrative-driven behavior. In the absence of clear information, investors create stories to explain short-term price movements. These stories, often fueled by fear or speculation, can feel rational in the moment but are rarely grounded in the long-term realities of the business.

Instincts that made sense historically don't always serve us today. Short-term uncertainty is no longer a threat to survival. Uncertainty can often be an opportunity. The recent share price fluctuations in our Liquidia investment, described below, provides a timely example.

Liquidia Therapeutics (LQDA)

Liquidia has navigated a complex landscape for years, marked by patent disputes and FDA approval delays for their flagship drug, Yutrepia. Today,everything is about Yutrepia's product launch and their prospects to penetrate a previously monopolistic market. For Liquidia, that uncertainty has always been a buying opportunity. Shares are 200% higher today than our original purchase price more than three years ago.

Yutrepia finally received FDA approval for Yutrepia in late May and officially launched soon thereafter. Interestingly, despite this significant milestone, Liquidia's stock price experienced a dramatic but temporary dip, falling from over$19 in early June to$12.46 by the end of the second quarter. This decline was primarily driven by investor apprehension regarding the initial commercial rollout and concerns about a potential competitor still years away from market entry. Since then, the narrative has rapidly changed again and today shares trade for nearly$18.

Initial reports for Yutrepia's June prescription numbers are exceptionally strong and well above expectations. While it's still early days, this robust start validates our confidence in Liquidia management's sales force and their go-to-market strategy, confirming the significant unmet need Yutrepia addresses in the market.

I first highlighted Liquidia in our Q4 2022 letter. The issues then were a variety of patent disputes with competitor United Therapeutics (UTHR). To date, United Therapeutics has failed in each of those legal cases but had succeeded in delaying Liquidia's launch and FDA approval. Lawfare worked extremely well in this situation.

There are still two long-shot legal challenges from United Therapuertics. Both of these cases are highly likely to be resolved in Liquidia's favor. But, as we have seen in this long saga, anything can happen. Fortunately, with Yutrepia now on the market, these legal tactics are far less impactful than before.

Beyond legal issues, Liquidia shares had dropped in early June due to the announcement of a successful phase two trial from a potential future competitor. On June 10, 2025, a company named Insmed (INSM) announced outstanding results for its experimental PAH therapy. Both Liquidia and United Therapeutics shares dropped in response while Insmed shares rose more than 40%. Insmed's drug,TPIP, is a dry powder treprostinil drug administrated via an inhaler and designed to be a once-daily treatment. As a reminder, Liquidia's Yutrepia delivers treprostinil via an inhaler three to five times a day. Obviously, all things being equal, a once daily treatment would be much more attractive to patients, which is why the results hurt Liquidia's stock price.

However, a closer look, as pointed out by a Raymond James (RJF) analyst, reveals potential issues with Insmed's trial design that may have artificially inflated the results. The analyst noted that the trial patients presented with more severe cases and appeared "undertreated at baseline versus key PAH studies, potentially favoring treatment response." This raises questions about the comparability and true efficacy of Insmed's findings.

It's worth considering the timing of Insmed's $750 million stock offering following these Phase 2 results and whether that fundraising prospect played into the design of the trial. Regardless, Insmed still needs to conduct a Phase 3 trial, which isn't expected to begin until next year, followed by an FDA approval requirement. Even assuming a best-case scenario with strong Phase 3 results, TPIP is unlikely to reach the market for many years. By then, Liquidia's next-generation drug, L606, which has also shown very strong clinical trial results to date, is expected to be available.

Is Insmed simply telling a good story at the moment, or are they are future threat to Liquidia? Only time will tell but my initial perspective is one of guarded skepticism.

In the meantime, I am continuing to monitor Liquidia's Yutrepia launch. We have been rewarded for our patience over the past three years, despite several bouts of volatility. I continue to be optimistic in the company, its leadership, and the prospects for its stock.

ENDI Corp (OTCQB:ENDI)

ENDI's operational performance continues to be exceptionally strong, particularly within its CrossingBridge subsidiary. As of June 30, CrossingBridge's Assets Under Management (AUM) surpassed $4 billion, a substantial increase from$3.4 billion at the start of the year. The subsidiary publicly reports AUM on their website on a monthly basis at crossingbridgeadvisors.com

Earlier this year, ENDI sold 25% of CrossingBridge at a $104 million valuation. Consequently, you can think of ENDI's share of that current AUM to be $3 billion (75% of$4 billion). ENDI also has approximately $52 million in cash and investments as of the March 31, 2025 reporting period and adjusting for the post-quarter partial subsidiary sale. The company also carries $10 million of debt. Their current market cap is $85 million.

At the current stock price of$16 per share, the market is valuing that$3 billion of growing AUM at$43 million. If we adjust for the valuation of the partial subsidiary sale, the market is pricing the CrossingBridge asset significantly lower than what sophisticated third-party investors paid earlier this year when AUM was 15% lower.

The bottom line is that we continue to have a long way to go to for ENDI to get to fair value. And, as the company continues to perform well operationally, that fair value continues to increase as well.

Uncertainty often presents the greatest opportunities. Short-term price movements do not always reflect a company's true value. And, remember, not all stories are true.

Thank you again for your long-term commitment to Arquitos. It has been a pleasure managing the fund for more than 13 years and investing on your behalf. I look forward to doing so for decades to come.

Best regards,

Steven Kiel

data.title: Fed Governor Waller Urges July Rate Cut
data.description:
Christopher J. Waller says the central bank should not wait until autumn to ease policy. Speaking to the Money Marketeers of New York University on July 17th, he argued for a 25-basis point trim to the federal funds target range “in two weeks.” With growth fizzling, hiring sputtering, and tariffs muddying the inflation picture, Waller believes quick action would keep the Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) from “falling behind the curve.”

The fed funds band of 4.25%–4.5% now sits a hefty 125–150 basis points above the Committee’s own 3% “neutral” estimate — terrain Waller openly calls restrictive. GDP expanded at barely a 1% annual rate in the first half of 2025, down from 2.8% late last year, while real consumer spending has slowed to the same anemic 1% clip. The July 16th Beige Book showed only “slight or modest gains” in five of twelve Fed Districts, with seven reporting flat or declining activity. Soft June retail sales underline a household sector that, after two years of real-wage erosion, finally seems to be buckling.

Labor, the lone bright spot of 2023-24, is wobbling as well. Headline unemployment is 4.1%, yet private-sector payrolls grew just 74,000 in June and may be “much closer to zero” after coming benchmark revisions, according to Waller. ADP data already show a 33,000 job decline, and the New York Fed pegs the unemployment rate for recent college grads at a decade-high 5.8%. Waller labels the market “on the edge”: hiring is weak even though layoffs remain subdued — often a precursor to broader job cuts. Waiting until September, he warns, risks tightening into an economy that can ill afford it.

Inflation gives the dovish faction cover. June numbers imply headline PCE at 2.5% and core at 2.7%, but roughly 0.3 percentage points stem from one-time tariff spikes. Stripping those out, Fed staff argue underlying inflation is “quite close” to the 2% target, helped by slower wage gains and better productivity. Most FOMC members already penciled in at least two 2025 cuts in June’s projections; acting in July leaves room to pause later if the data misbehave. As Waller put it, “For this reason, I believe it makes sense to cut the FOMC’s policy rate by 25 basis points two weeks from now.”

Whether or not the Committee pulls the trigger on July 30th, the backdrop is unmistakable: growth stalling, labor momentum fading, and inflation progress hinging on spreadsheets that exclude politically driven tariffs. In that environment, the yellow metal’s new highs may be telling a story policymakers would rather not hear.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Investors Bailing Out Of U.S. Stocks?
data.description:
In the Investment Company Institute's release of weekly fund and ETF flows on July 23, 2025, the report showed a significant outflow in U.S. equities. The report shows a $299.5 billion outflow from U.S. equities.

In reviewing the data back to early 2016, this outflow is significantly larger than any past outflow reported. Next week one will see if this report is revised in any way, but for now, given the nearly uninterrupted advance in the U.S. equity market, investors might want to take note of this data point.

ICI does note, "The outflows from domestic equity mutual funds for the week ending July 16 were primarily driven by activity in collective investment trusts, rather than by redemptions from individual investors." These represent outflows nonetheless.

data.title: Rates Spark: Path To Higher Euro Rates Is Almost Cleared
data.description:
By Michiel Tukker

More and more support for higher euro rates

ECB President Christine Lagarde happily emphasised that the European Central Bank was in a 'good place', giving a signal that a cut in September will probably not be necessary. Markets reduced the probability of a September cut from 50% to 25%, although they seem to agree that the ECB will have to cut to 1.75% eventually. The key uncertainty remains the ongoing trade tensions, which still bear the risk of escalation involving retaliatory measures from both sides.

After good PMI numbers and a positive tone from the ECB, the path towards higher rates is becoming even clearer. If a trade deal gets signed, we should see rates move up further, with the 10Y swap rate hitting at least 2.8%. The stretch towards 3% should be slower and will be contingent on growth numbers continuing the upward trend. Passing the 3% mark would probably require the impact from fiscal stimulus to show in the data, which may not be until later in 2026.

On the other hand, we do also have to deal with disinflationary risks, partly because of base effects and partly because of the stronger euro. The ECB is opting to look through these and focus on the projections, but the question is whether markets can do the same. The 10Y inflation swap stands at exactly 2%, which is still on the low side when considering an inflation risk premium of around 30bp. This signals that markets remain wary that maintaining inflation at target is not a done deal.

Friday’s events and market views

From Germany, we'll first have the Ifo survey results, which should support the improving business sentiment. The ECB will publish the numbers from the survey of professional forecasts, but these are usually not market-moving. Later in the day, we have the durable goods orders coming in from the US, which will be watched closely for signs of weakness.
